topic: The Impact of Habitat Loss on Species
title: Habitat Loss and Biodiversity: A Threat to Global Ecosystems

lesson.learn: Habitat loss is the primary driver of species extinction, accounting for 80-85% of recorded extinctions since 1500 AD. Between 1970 and 2016, the world lost nearly 13 million hectares of forest, equivalent to an area roughly the size of South Korea. The Amazon rainforest, home to over 10% of all known plant and animal species, has lost a staggering 17% of its forest cover between 2000 and 2018. Habitat loss due to deforestation, urbanization, and agriculture has severe consequences for ecosystems, including reduced genetic diversity, population decline, and ecosystem collapse.
lesson.simpler: Habitat loss is a significant threat to global biodiversity. When natural habitats are destroyed or degraded, species are left without a place to live, feed, or breed, ultimately leading to population decline and extinction. Human activities, such as deforestation, urbanization, and agriculture, are the primary drivers of habitat loss.
lesson.keyTakeaway: The key takeaway is that habitat loss is a critical component of the biodiversity crisis, driving species extinction and ecosystem collapse. Understanding the impact of human activities on natural habitats is essential for mitigating the effects of habitat loss and preserving biodiversity.
lesson.deeperDive: Research has shown that habitat loss is the primary driver of species extinction, accounting for 80-85% of recorded extinctions since 1500 AD. The majority of these extinctions occurred in the past century, with the most notable declines occurring in the 1980s. The Amazon rainforest, which accounts for 40% of the world's remaining tropical forests, is a prime example of habitat loss due to human activities. Between 2000 and 2018, the Amazon lost a staggering 17% of its forest cover, primarily due to deforestation and agriculture. The Congo Basin, which is often referred to as the 'second lung of the Earth,' has lost an estimated 40% of its primary forest cover in just a few decades, equivalent to an area roughly the size of the United Kingdom.
lesson.funFact: The world's coral reefs cover only 0.1% of the ocean's surface but are home to over 25% of all marine species. Habitat loss due to climate change, overfishing, and pollution has resulted in the loss of over 30% of the world's coral reef cover between 1970 and 2016.

people: - Rachel Carson (Marine biologist and conservationist who highlighted the impact of human activities on ecosystems)
- Jane Goodall (Primatologist and anthropologist known for her groundbreaking research on chimpanzees and conservation efforts)
- Chandi Prasad Bhatt (Indian environmental activist who led the Chipko movement to save forests)
- Wangari Maathai (Kenyan environmentalist and politician who founded the Green Belt Movement)
- David Attenborough (British naturalist and broadcaster who has dedicated his life to exploring and protecting our planet's ecosystems)
places: - Amazon Rainforest (The largest tropical rainforest in the world, spanning across nine countries in South America)
- Congo Basin (The second-largest tropical rainforest in the world, covering an area of over 2 million square kilometers)
- Serengeti National Park (A UNESCO World Heritage Site and home to the Big Five African animals: lion, leopard, rhinoceros, elephant, and Cape buffalo)
- Australian Great Barrier Reef (One of the most biodiverse ecosystems on the planet, facing significant threats from climate change and pollution)
- Yellowstone National Park (The first national park in the world, known for its geothermal wonders and diverse wildlife)
years: - 1986: The World Wildlife Fund launches its Species programme to focus on conservation efforts worldwide
- 1992: The United Nations convenes the Earth Summit in Rio de Janeiro, highlighting the importance of sustainable development and environmental protection
- 2000: The Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species of Wild Fauna and Flora (CITES) is established to regulate the trade of threatened species
- 2015: The Paris Agreement sets a global goal to limit climate change to 1.5°C above pre-industrial levels, highlighting the need for urgent action on environmental issues
concepts: - Climate Change and its impact on ecosystems
